batchMeta.batchTitle: Microbiology chain, pharm intro, PK/PD, IV calculations
curriculumNotes: Assumes cell-structure-and-function (batch 3), organic molecules (batch 3), and oral dosage (batch 3) already in learner path.
Pharmacokinetics includes digestive/urinary bridge; full anatomy topics still recommended.
Pharmacodynamics follows PK per blueprint ordering in this file.

structuredContent.overview: Bacteria, viruses, fungi, and parasites differ in structure, treatment targets, and infection control implications.
groups: Bacteria: prokaryotic cells; treated with antibiotics when appropriate.
Viruses: obligate intracellular parasites; need host cell machinery; antivirals target specific steps.
Fungi: eukaryotes; include yeasts and molds; antifungals for serious fungal disease.
Parasites: protozoa and helminths (worm) categories at survey level.
commonMistakes: Calling viral illness 'bacterial' and expecting antibiotics
structuredContent.clinicalRelevanceLight: Antibiotic stewardship starts with recognizing when bacteria are not the cause.

lessons.title: Prokaryote vs eukaryote and why it matters for drugs

structuredContent.overview: Bacteria are prokaryotes without a nucleus; human cells are eukaryotic. Many antibiotics exploit differences in bacterial structures (e.g., cell wall) or machinery.
structuredContent.mentalModel: Target the pathogen without destroying human cells—selective toxicity is the goal of many antimicrobials.
structuredContent.clinicalRelevanceLight: Side effects still happen when human biology overlaps with drug targets (preview).

options.B: bacterium alone in broth always same as human cell
options.D: erythrocyte without nucleus as model for all

options.B: have cell walls like plants always

options.A: common with Staph species among normal flora (survey)

lessons.title: Bacterial cell envelope and Gram stain logic

structuredContent.overview: Gram-positive bacteria retain crystal violet due to thick peptidoglycan; gram-negative have thinner peptidoglycan and outer membrane with endotoxin (LPS) implications.
concepts: Gram stain: crystal violet, iodine, decolorizer, safranin
commonMistakes: Thinking Gram reaction predicts antibiotic choice alone—it informs, labs guide
structuredContent.clinicalRelevanceLight: Gram stain is rapid clue; cultures refine therapy.

structuredContent.overview: Binary fission doubles population when conditions allow. Resistance emerges via selection pressure and genetic change.
structuredContent.clinicalRelevanceLight: Finish prescribed courses when ordered; stewardship reduces resistance spread.

structuredContent.overview: Viruses contain genetic material (DNA or RNA) inside a capsid; some have an envelope derived from host membrane.
structuredContent.clinicalRelevanceLight: Hand hygiene and vaccines target transmission and immunity, not 'antibiotics'.

structuredContent.overview: Attachment, entry, replication of genome, assembly, release—details vary by virus.
concepts: Lytic vs lysogenic in bacteriophage teaching
Antivirals may block entry, replication, or release steps
structuredContent.clinicalRelevanceLight: Exposure protocols and isolation depend on transmission route (contact, droplet, airborne).

options.B: bacterial ribosome 70S always as sole target

options.A: genome persists with periodic reactivation possible (survey)
host cell lysis releasing new virions (survey)
phage DNA integrated into bacterial chromosome (survey)

options.B: smallpox virus as DNA example contrast

options.A: stimulates immunity to prevent or lessen disease
tiny particles that remain suspended (survey examples later)
given to prevent infection/outbreak in defined contexts

structuredContent.overview: Narrow-spectrum drugs hit fewer organism types; broad-spectrum hit more but disturb flora and resistance risk more.
structuredContent.clinicalRelevanceLight: Culture and sensitivity guide narrowing therapy when possible.

structuredContent.overview: Antivirals block specific viral steps; antifungals often target ergosterol-rich membranes or cell wall (echinocandins) at advanced levels.
structuredContent.clinicalRelevanceLight: Drug choice follows diagnosis, not convenience.

structuredContent.overview: Finish courses when prescribed; do not share antibiotics; report allergies accurately.
concepts: MRSA, VRE, ESBL as acronyms to recognize—not memorize full mechanisms yet
structuredContent.clinicalRelevanceLight: Infection control plus appropriate prescribing slows resistance.

options.A: drug no longer effectively kills or inhibits the bacterium

options.B: always safer in every case without thought

options.A: broad antibiotic exposure disrupting colon flora (survey)

questions.stem: Bactericidal vs bacteriostatic at student level:

options.C: intermediate always means give double always

options.A: prevent infection in specific situations per protocol
treat before culture results using likely coverage
accurate; many labeled are not true IgE allergy

questions.stem: Drug interaction with warfarin and antibiotics can affect:

options.A: affect QT and interact with other drugs (recognition)
renal function and potassium in some patients (recognition)
serotonin interaction risk with certain meds (recognition)

options.C: only PO for all infections same

options.A: interfere with viral nucleic acid synthesis (survey)
antiseptic on living tissue surfaces reduce microbes; systemic antibiotics internal
disinfectant on objects; antiseptic on skin (survey)
harm pathogen more than host at therapeutic doses (goal)
certain beta-lactam + aminoglycoside pairings in specific infections (preview)
anaerobic bacterial and certain protozoal coverage (recognition)

topics.topicName: Introduction to Pharmacology: Drug Names and Classifications

lessons.title: Generic, brand, and why both names appear

structuredContent.overview: Generic is the nonproprietary name; brand is marketed by a company. Orders should be clear; double-check look-alike/sound-alike pairs.
structuredContent.clinicalRelevanceLight: Barcode systems often key off generic plus strength/form.

structuredContent.overview: A class shares mechanism or use; a prototype drug is the classic teaching example (e.g., propranolol for beta blockers at survey).
structuredContent.clinicalRelevanceLight: Class effects predict side effects (e.g., beta blockers and heart rate).

structuredContent.overview: Schedule I–V reflect abuse potential and regulation increasing toward Schedule I (non-medical use in schedule I classic teaching).
structuredContent.note: Canada and other countries differ; follow your jurisdiction.
structuredContent.clinicalRelevanceLight: Counts, wasting, and witness rules apply to controlled meds.

options.A: over-the-counter without prescription in many contexts
classic teaching example for a class

questions.stem: DEA schedule I at classic US teaching:

options.A: high abuse potential; no accepted medical use in US teaching
high abuse potential; tight prescribing rules (survey)
similar expected effect at same dose (regulatory standard)
approved or prescribed reason for use
situation where drug should not be used
use not in official labeling though may be evidence-based

questions.stem: Biologic vs small molecule at survey:

options.A: large complex molecules vs traditional pills often
regulated differently than drugs in US (recognition)
increased harm if error (ISMP list concept)
often high-risk drug class needing safeguards
high bleed risk class needing vigilance
controlled with abuse and respiratory risk
oversedation and falls especially in elderly

questions.stem: A patient asks why generic looks different; best student response framework:

options.A: same active ingredient; excipients/color may differ per policy teaching
options.B: say it is a different drug always

topics.topicName: Pharmacokinetics: Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, and Excretion
topics.prerequisiteBridgingNote: Lesson 1 embeds GI and renal context for absorption/excretion; complete full A&P digestive/urinary topics in curriculum.

structuredContent.overview: Absorption depends on route, blood flow, solubility, and barriers.

prerequisiteBridgeDigestiveUrinary.GItract: Oral drugs traverse GI mucosa; stomach acid, food, motility, and first-pass metabolism alter bioavailability.
prerequisiteBridgeDigestiveUrinary.KidneyRoleExcretion: Many drugs and metabolites exit via renal filtration and secretion; hydration and kidney function matter.

concepts: First-pass effect reduces oral bioavailability for some drugs
structuredContent.clinicalRelevanceLight: NPO status, vomiting, and tube feeds can change absorption.

structuredContent.overview: Drug distributes by blood flow, tissue affinity, and protein binding (especially albumin).
concepts: Unbound drug is often considered active at simple teaching level
structuredContent.clinicalRelevanceLight: Low albumin can change free drug for highly bound agents (preview).

structuredContent.overview: Liver enzymes (notably cytochrome P450 family) chemically modify drugs to often more water-soluble metabolites.
concepts: Enzyme inducers/inhibitors change other drug levels (preview)
structuredContent.clinicalRelevanceLight: Grapefruit and some antibiotics alter metabolism of certain drugs.

lessons.title: Excretion: mainly renal; half-life and steady state

structuredContent.overview: Kidneys excrete many drugs; half-life is time for amount to fall by half (student definition). Steady state approaches after ~4–5 half-lives of regular dosing.
concepts: Dose adjustments in renal impairment for some drugs
structuredContent.clinicalRelevanceLight: Creatinine clearance guides dosing for renally cleared drugs in practice.

options.A: movement from administration site into circulation
liver metabolism before systemic circulation for oral drugs
complete for the delivered dose into vein
limit free drug available to tissues (concept)
major drug-metabolizing enzyme family in liver (survey)
increase levels of substrates of that enzyme
decrease levels of substrates over time
time for drug amount to decrease by half (student definition)

questions.stem: Steady state with repeated dosing approaches after:
Loading dose may be used to:

options.A: reach therapeutic effect faster for some drugs
amount eliminated per dosing interval at steady state (concept)
reduced renal function and body composition changes
therapeutic drug monitoring for narrow-index drugs (recognition)
assess toxicity and subtherapeutic risk for some drugs
faster than SC for many drugs (general)
increase or decrease absorption depending on drug
ionization and dissolution of some drugs

questions.stem: Small intestine is major site for:
Unbound drug concept at simple teaching:

options.A: often correlates with effect for highly bound drugs discussion
options.C: only bound drug active always wrong simplification noted

options.A: blood flow limited or enzyme limited (preview)
fixed amount per time at high concentrations (alcohol classic preview)
relates drug amount in body to plasma concentration (concept)
protect drug from gastric acid or protect stomach (survey)
smooth plasma levels / reduce dosing frequency
free fraction of highly protein-bound drugs (concept)
unabsorbed oral drug or biliary route (concept)
what the body does to the drug

structuredContent.overview: Drugs bind targets; affinity is tightness of binding; efficacy is ability to activate effect. Dose-response curves show increasing effect with dose until plateau.
structuredContent.clinicalRelevanceLight: Explains why more drug is not always more effect past ceiling.

structuredContent.overview: Therapeutic index compares toxic dose to effective dose in classic teaching (TD50/ED50 style). Narrow index means smaller margin.
structuredContent.clinicalRelevanceLight: Digoxin, lithium, theophylline classic examples requiring vigilance.

structuredContent.overview: Repeated exposure can reduce response (tolerance); stopping some drugs causes rebound; opioids have dependence risk with public health impact.
structuredContent.clinicalRelevanceLight: Teach patients not to stop beta blockers abruptly without prescriber guidance (example class).

options.A: what the drug does to the body
activates but submaximal effect vs full agonist
maximal effect without occupying all receptors
dose needed for defined effect; more potent needs lower dose
margin between helpful and harmful doses
affects intended target more than others at therapeutic doses (goal)
reduced response with prolonged agonist exposure
dose producing 50% of maximal effect in model
range between subtherapeutic and toxic for many drugs

questions.stem: Receptor antagonist overdose of agonist might be:

options.A: binds non-active site changing receptor behavior (preview)
drug used to provoke response for diagnosis (recognition)
rapidly diminishing response after repeated doses (recognition)
lack of response despite adequate dose (contextual)
reversible at student level for many drugs

questions.stem: Intrinsic activity of agonist relates to:

options.B: only shift right with same max

options.A: two drugs oppose via different mechanisms

topics.topicName: IV Flow Rate and Drip Rate Calculations

commonMistakes: Forgetting to convert hour to minute

structuredContent.overview: Time = total volume / rate. Verify pump programming; double-check high-risk meds.
structuredContent.clinicalRelevanceLight: Rate errors with IV can cause rapid harm; use protocols.
